Course Title: AI in Cloud Environments: Shared Responsibility Gaps
Category: Technology & Telecommunications: Information Technology, Science & Technology, Software, Hardware, Telecom
Description: Misunderstandings between provider and customer responsibilities.
Prerequisites: Learners should have a basic familiarity with cloud computing concepts (such as storage, servers, and online services) and a general awareness of what artificial intelligence means in everyday technology contexts.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the key shared responsibility boundaries between cloud providers and customers in AI environments, and explain how common misunderstandings arise across security, data management, and compliance. You will also apply practical frameworks to clarify accountability gaps, helping you to organise responsibilities effectively and reduce risk within your organisation's AI and cloud programmes.

lessons.lesson title: AI in Cloud Environments: Shared Responsibility Gaps
lessons.Description: When organisations move AI workloads into the cloud, it is easy to assume that the cloud provider handles all aspects of security and compliance. In reality, responsibility is divided between the provider and the customer, and misunderstanding where one ends and the other begins can leave serious gaps in protection. This lesson introduces the shared responsibility model as it applies specifically to AI environments, helping you understand which safeguards your cloud provider manages and which ones your organisation must put in place. Using clear, practical examples, you will explore common misconceptions that lead to vulnerabilities, data exposure, and compliance failures. By the end of this lesson, you will have a solid foundation for identifying and addressing the responsibility gaps that are most likely to affect your AI deployments.
lessons.content: Misunderstandings between provider and customer responsibilities.

slides.subtitle: Navigating the blurred lines of accountability
slides.slidecontent:
• Cloud AI services split responsibilities between the provider and the customer
• Providers secure the infrastructure
• Customers must protect their data, configurations, and access controls
• Many breaches occur in the gap between what each side assumes the other is handling
• Shared responsibility sounds simple but causes real confusion in practice

slides.subtitle: Understanding the foundation
slides.slidecontent:
• Provider owns physical infrastructure, servers, and networking
• Customer owns data security, applications, and user access
• Responsibility is not split equally down the middle
• The word 'shared' misleads many organisations
• Classic model works well for traditional cloud services

slides.subtitle: Why traditional boundaries blur
slides.slidecontent:
• AI tools span multiple layers of the cloud stack
• Models process customer data but run on provider infrastructure
• External API calls create new dependency chains
• Traditional categories no longer fit cleanly
• Nobody explicitly claims ownership of emerging risks

slides.subtitle: Who protects what you deploy?
slides.slidecontent:
• Provider infrastructure security does not automatically cover your models
• Fine-tuned and custom models often fall outside standard coverage
• Model behaviour and accountability rarely addressed in contracts
• Customers frequently assume full protection is included
• The model layer becomes an unmanaged risk

slides.subtitle: The grey areas that cause incidents
slides.slidecontent:
• Logging and monitoring ownership varies by service and provider
• Access controls may be customer-managed even for provider-hosted models
• Misconfigurations go unnoticed when responsibility is unclear
• Neither side actively monitors the middle ground
• Small oversights become serious vulnerabilities over time

slides.subtitle: Dangerous assumptions organisations make
slides.slidecontent:
• 'My cloud provider handles all security'
• 'Shared responsibility means equal responsibility'
• 'Standard contracts cover AI-specific risks'
• 'Infrastructure security equals application security'
• 'One-time configuration is sufficient'

slides.subtitle: Practical steps to bridge the gap
slides.slidecontent:
• Audit your cloud AI setup to identify which controls fall under your responsibility
• Document ownership clearly
• Use your provider's shared responsibility matrix as a starting point, not a final answer
• Train your team on the specific gaps that apply to your AI workloads
• Review configurations regularly, as AI services evolve and responsibilities can shift
• Treat responsibility mapping as an ongoing process, not a one-time task

slides.subtitle: What you must keep in mind
slides.slidecontent:
• Cloud AI security is always a shared responsibility between you and your provider
• Gaps appear when neither side assumes ownership of a specific task
• Know your layer
• Review your provider's shared responsibility model before deploying any AI workload
• Misconfigurations, not breaches, are the most common cause of cloud AI incidents
• Start by mapping which controls you own and which your provider covers

scripts.script: When you use AI services in the cloud, there is an unspoken agreement happening in the background about who is responsible for keeping things secure. The cloud provider takes care of the underlying infrastructure, the servers, the networking, the physical hardware. But everything above that, your data, your settings, your access controls, that part falls to you as the customer. And here is where things get interesting, and honestly, where things go wrong. Both sides often assume the other is handling something, and in that gap, breaches happen. It is not that the model is broken, it is that it is widely misunderstood. People hear 'shared responsibility' and think it sounds straightforward, but in practice it creates real confusion, especially when AI services are involved, because they add extra layers that do not always fit neatly into the traditional model.

Before we dive into AI specifically, we need to understand the foundation everything else is built on: the traditional shared responsibility model. In classic cloud services, the provider secures the physical infrastructure, servers, networking, and the underlying platform. That part is their job. Your job is securing the data, applications, and user access. The word 'shared' can be misleading though. It does not mean responsibility is split equally down the middle. This traditional model actually works quite well for straightforward cloud services like storage or basic computing. But as we will see, AI introduces complications that the original model was not designed to handle.

Here is where AI fundamentally changes the game. Unlike traditional applications that sit clearly in one layer, AI tools span multiple levels of the cloud stack. Your model processes customer data but runs on provider infrastructure. It might call external APIs, creating new dependency chains that were not there before. Suddenly the clean lines between 'their job' and 'your job' start to blur significantly. Traditional responsibility categories simply do not fit AI workloads cleanly. What happens is that new types of risks emerge, ones that nobody has explicitly claimed ownership of. When nobody owns a risk, it goes unmanaged, and that is exactly where problems begin.

Here is where things get genuinely complex. Many customers assume that because their AI model is running on a secure cloud platform, the model itself is fully protected. That assumption can be dangerous. Your provider does an excellent job securing the underlying infrastructure, but that infrastructure security does not automatically extend to your model's behaviour or configuration. If you have fine-tuned a model or built something custom, there is a very good chance your provider's standard protections simply do not cover it. Contracts rarely address model behaviour and accountability either. So if something goes wrong with your AI model, who is actually responsible? Often, nobody has clearly claimed that ownership.

Between what the provider handles and what you handle, there is a grey area, and that grey area is where problems quietly take root. Take logging and monitoring, for instance. Sometimes the provider handles those, sometimes it falls to you, and sometimes nobody has clearly claimed ownership at all. Access controls can be particularly tricky because even with provider-hosted models, you might still be responsible for managing who can access what. When responsibility is unclear, misconfigurations slip through unnoticed. Nobody spots them because everyone assumes someone else is watching. Those small oversights can sit there for months, growing into serious security vulnerabilities.

Let us talk about the dangerous assumptions that get organisations into trouble. The most common one is thinking that your cloud provider handles all aspects of security. Another is believing that 'shared responsibility' means the workload is split equally. Many people also assume that standard cloud contracts automatically cover AI-specific risks, but they rarely do. There is also this misconception that if the infrastructure is secure, everything running on top of it must be secure too. And finally, many organisations treat security configuration as a one-time setup, when in reality it needs ongoing attention. These misconceptions are not just theoretical problems, they lead to real vulnerabilities in live systems.

So now that we understand where the gaps exist, let's talk about what you can actually do about them. The first step is to audit your current cloud AI setup and get clear on which controls are genuinely your responsibility. Use your provider's shared responsibility matrix as a starting point, but don't treat it as the complete picture, because it rarely is. Once you know what falls on your side, document it clearly and make sure the right people own the right areas. It's also worth training your team specifically on the gaps that apply to your AI workloads, not just general cloud security. And here's something that catches a lot of organisations off guard: responsibilities can shift over time as AI services evolve and providers update their offerings. So this isn't something you do once and forget. Treat responsibility mapping as a living process, something you revisit regularly to make sure nothing slips through the cracks.

Let us bring together the most important ideas from this lesson. The first thing to hold onto is that cloud AI security is never the responsibility of just one party. It is always shared between you and your provider, and the danger zone is wherever that line is unclear. When nobody has explicitly claimed ownership of a control, that is where gaps appear, and that is where incidents happen. And speaking of incidents, it is worth knowing that most cloud AI security problems are not caused by sophisticated attacks. They are caused by misconfigurations, simple mistakes that could have been avoided. The practical step you can take right now is to go and review your provider's shared responsibility model before you deploy anything. Then map it out clearly: which controls sit with you, and which sit with them. Once you know your layer, you can actually defend it. That clarity is really the foundation of everything we have covered today.

Who is this for: IT managers, cloud administrators, data managers, compliance officers, project managers overseeing AI initiatives, business analysts working with cloud services
Team or Dept this is for: IT, Operations, Compliance, Risk Management, Data Management
